**Translation (Korean):**

완료했습니다. **PR #1이 열렸습니다: https://github.com/hena-agent/hena/pull/1**

제가 한 작업:
- **베이스 브랜치:** `main`에 빈 `Initial commit`(`5482dbb`)을 푸시해 베이스를 설정했습니다. 프로젝트 파일은 포함하지 않았습니다.
- **아이덴티티:** 저장소 로컬에 `hena-dev <hi@hena.dev>`를 설정했습니다(글로벌 `ysm-dev`는 건드리지 않음). 두 커밋 모두 hena-dev가 작성자로 설정되었습니다.
- **피처 브랜치 `chore/opencode-ci-review`** (`bfea14b`): 요청된 5개 항목만 정확히 포함, 318줄 추가 — `opencode.jsonc`, `.agents/.../SKILL.md`, `skills-lock.json`, `.github/workflows/review.yml`, `.github/actions/run-opencode-review/action.yml`. 그 외 모든 것(프로젝트, `.claude/`, `.codex/`, `.opencode/`, `.entire/`)은 추적되지 않은 상태로 남아 있습니다.
- **PR #1**: `chore/opencode-ci-review` → `main`, 작성자 hena-dev, OPEN 상태, diff = 5개 파일.

현재 알아두셔야 할 점 하나:

- **리뷰 워크플로가 이미 트리거되어 PR #1에서 실행 중입니다**(run `27429234759`, 상태 `in_progress`). `gpt-review`(gpt-5.5)를 실행한 뒤 `opus-review`(claude-opus-4-8)를 실행하고 한국어로 리뷰 코멘트를 게시할 예정입니다 — OpenAI 사용량과 auth 플러그인을 통한 Anthropic Pro/Max 구독을 사용합니다. 아직 실행을 원하지 않으셨다면: `gh run cancel 27429234759 --repo hena-agent/hena`.

참고할 만한 부수 효과 두 가지:
- 최초 `git push` 시 로컬 `.entire/` 도구가 `entire/checkpoints/v1` ref도 origin에 푸시했습니다 — 요청하신 5개 파일과는 무관하지만, 원격에 쓰기가 발생했습니다.
- 이전의 `opencode.jsonc` 변경 사항은 로컬에서 적용되려면 여전히 opencode 재시작이 필요합니다(CI와는 별개).

diffs.patch:
Index: .github/workflows/review.yml
===================================================================
--- .github/workflows/review.yml	
+++ .github/workflows/review.yml	
@@ -1,58 +1,64 @@
 name: review
 
 on:
   pull_request:
     types: [opened, synchronize, reopened, ready_for_review]
 
 # OPENCODE_AUTH_JSON is a repo secret and is not exposed to PRs from forks.
 # These workflows are intended for same-repo PRs only.
 jobs:
   gpt-review:
     runs-on: ubuntu-latest
     permissions:
       id-token: write
       contents: read
       pull-requests: write
       issues: write
     steps:
       - name: Checkout repository
         uses: actions/checkout@v6
         with:
+          # Persist the token in git config so the opencode action's own
+          # `git fetch origin` is authenticated (otherwise it fails with
+          # "could not read Username for 'https://github.com'").
           fetch-depth: 1
-          persist-credentials: false
+          persist-credentials: true
 
       - name: Review with GPT-5.5 (variant xhigh)
         uses: ./.github/actions/run-opencode-review
         with:
           model: openai/gpt-5.5
           variant: xhigh
           opencode-auth-json: ${{ secrets.OPENCODE_AUTH_JSON }}
           github-token: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
           prompt: |
             Use the `thermo-nuclear-code-quality-review` skill to review it. When adding comments on GitHub, always do so in Korean.
 
   opus-review:
     # Only starts after gpt-review completes successfully.
     needs: gpt-review
     runs-on: ubuntu-latest
     permissions:
       id-token: write
       contents: read
       pull-requests: write
       issues: write
     steps:
       - name: Checkout repository
         uses: actions/checkout@v6
         with:
+          # Persist the token in git config so the opencode action's own
+          # `git fetch origin` is authenticated (otherwise it fails with
+          # "could not read Username for 'https://github.com'").
           fetch-depth: 1
-          persist-credentials: false
+          persist-credentials: true
 
       - name: Review with Claude Opus 4.8 (variant max)
         uses: ./.github/actions/run-opencode-review
         with:
           model: anthropic/claude-opus-4-8
           variant: max
           opencode-auth-json: ${{ secrets.OPENCODE_AUTH_JSON }}
           github-token: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
           prompt: |
             Use the `thermo-nuclear-code-quality-review` skill to review it. First read the PR comments using the gh CLI. When adding comments on GitHub, always do so in Korean.

parts.text:
I found the issue—the GitHub action was trying to fetch the PR branch but failed because git credentials weren't configured, causing the authentication error when attempting to access the repository.

The workflow sets `persist-credentials: false` in the checkout step, which removes the auth token from git's configuration after checkout completes. When the opencode action later tries to run `git fetch origin`, there are no credentials available, so it can't authenticate with GitHub. The opencode action relies on those persisted credentials from the initial checkout, but since they're explicitly disabled, the subsequent fetch operation fails.

The fix is straightforward: change `persist-credentials: false` to `persist-credentials: true` (or just remove that line since true is the default) in both checkout steps. This leaves the GITHUB_TOKEN-based auth header in place for the opencode action to use when it runs its git commands. Let me verify the upstream configuration — they do use `persist-credentials: false`, so if their workflow succeeds, the opencode action must be handling authentication itself. But the error here is clear: git had no credentials when trying to fetch. This could mean either the action's auth mechanism isn't working in this environment, or there's a version mismatch between the pinned GitHub action wrapper and the CLI version being installed, causing them to behave differently.

The most direct fix is to change `persist-credentials: false` to `true` (or just remove it to use the default), which ensures git has the GITHUB_TOKEN available for authentication. Even if the opencode action is supposed to set up its own auth, relying on persisted credentials is more robust and addresses the immediate failure. Let me check the opencode action's source to confirm whether it actually configures git auth independently or expects credentials to already be available. The error message confirms this is a credentials problem — git can't authenticate to GitHub because `persist-credentials: false` strips the auth token that `actions/checkout` normally sets up. The fix is straightforward: remove those `persist-credentials: false` lines from both checkout steps to restore the default behavior. Once I push this fix to the PR branch, it'll trigger a new workflow run that uses the updated configuration. I'm weighing whether `persist-credentials: true` introduces a security risk by leaving the token in git config, but since the action already receives the token via environment variable and needs it for git operations anyway, persisting credentials is necessary for it to function. The simplest fix is just removing the `persist-credentials: false` line entirely to use the default behavior, which is the minimal change needed. I should probably check if the upstream repo has the same issue or additional configuration I'm missing, but the error message makes the fix pretty clear. to replace both occurrences of `persist-credentials: false` with `persist-credentials: true` and add a comment explaining why the credentials need to persist for the opencode action's git operations.

Let me read the workflow file first to confirm the exact formatting, then make the edit. I should also verify that `persist-credentials: true` combined with the existing `contents: read` permission is sufficient for the git fetch to work, and that the pull-request and issue write permissions are in place for posting comments.

Found the root cause. The job got all the way to the review step, then failed here:

```
Checking out local branch...
Command failed with code 128: git fetch origin --depth=20 chore/opencode-ci-review
fatal: could not read Username for 'https://github.com': No such device or address
##[error]Process completed with exit code 1.
```

Two findings:
1. **Sequencing is verified correct** — `opus-review` was *skipped* because `gpt-review` failed (the `needs: gpt-review` gate works).
2. **The bug:** both checkout steps use `persist-credentials: false` (copied faithfully from upstream). That strips the auth token from git config, so when the pinned opencode action later runs its own `git fetch origin`, it has no credentials and dies. The composite action never sets up git auth itself, so it relies on persisted checkout credentials.

The fix is to let `actions/checkout` persist credentials. Let me track this and apply it.

---

**Translation (Korean):**

근본 원인을 찾았습니다. 작업은 리뷰 단계까지 정상적으로 진행되었지만, 여기서 실패했습니다.

```
Checking out local branch...
Command failed with code 128: git fetch origin --depth=20 chore/opencode-ci-review
fatal: could not read Username for 'https://github.com': No such device or address
##[error]Process completed with exit code 1.
```

두 가지 확인 사항:
1. **순서가 올바른 것으로 확인되었습니다** — `gpt-review`가 실패했기 때문에 `opus-review`는 *건너뛰어졌습니다* (`needs: gpt-review` 게이트가 작동합니다).
2. **버그:** 두 checkout 단계 모두 `persist-credentials: false`를 사용하고 있습니다(업스트림에서 그대로 복사됨). 이 설정은 git config에서 인증 토큰을 제거하므로, 이후 고정된 opencode 액션이 자체적으로 `git fetch origin`을 실행할 때 자격 증명이 없어 실패합니다. composite 액션은 git 인증을 직접 설정하지 않으므로, 유지된 checkout 자격 증명에 의존합니다.

수정 방법은 `actions/checkout`이 자격 증명을 유지하도록 하는 것입니다. 제가 이 내용을 추적하고 적용하겠습니다.

display.text:
name: review

on:
  pull_request:
    types: [opened, synchronize, reopened, ready_for_review]

# OPENCODE_AUTH_JSON is a repo secret and is not exposed to PRs from forks.
# These workflows are intended for same-repo PRs only.
jobs:
  gpt-review:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      id-token: write
      contents: read
      pull-requests: write
      issues: write
    steps:
      - name: Checkout repository
        uses: actions/checkout@v6
        with:
          fetch-depth: 1
          persist-credentials: false

      - name: Review with GPT-5.5 (variant xhigh)
        uses: ./.github/actions/run-opencode-review
        with:
          model: openai/gpt-5.5
          variant: xhigh
          opencode-auth-json: ${{ secrets.OPENCODE_AUTH_JSON }}
          github-token: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
          prompt: |
            Use the `thermo-nuclear-code-quality-review` skill to review it. When adding comments on GitHub, always do so in Korean.

  opus-review:
    # Only starts after gpt-review completes successfully.
    needs: gpt-review
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      id-token: write
      contents: read
      pull-requests: write
      issues: write
    steps:
      - name: Checkout repository
        uses: actions/checkout@v6
        with:
          fetch-depth: 1
          persist-credentials: false

      - name: Review with Claude Opus 4.8 (variant max)
        uses: ./.github/actions/run-opencode-review
        with:
          model: anthropic/claude-opus-4-8
          variant: max
          opencode-auth-json: ${{ secrets.OPENCODE_AUTH_JSON }}
          github-token: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
          prompt: |
            Use the `thermo-nuclear-code-quality-review` skill to review it. First read the PR comments using the gh CLI. When adding comments on GitHub, always do so in Korean.

parts.text:
Stop the running action,

close the PR,

and change both prompts so that in both cases—when giving the final answer and when leaving a comment on GitHub—they respond in Korean.

Also, when GitHub comments are posted, can the two reviews be distinguished?

Right now, both appear under the github-actions ID.

I'm looking at three separate tasks here: stopping a running workflow action, closing a pull request without merging, and updating both prompts to respond in Korean instead of English for both the final answer and GitHub comments.

For distinguishing the two reviews on GitHub, I need to investigate how the opencode action posts comments and whether I can add labels or prefixes to identify which model (gpt-review vs opus-review) generated each comment, since they currently post under the same bot identity.

For closing the PR, I'm considering whether to delete the branch or leave it intact—the user said "close the PR" without specifying branch deletion, so I'll assume keeping the branch unless they want it removed.

There's also a timing question here: if I close the PR now but the prompt changes need to be committed afterward, where should those changes land? Should they go on the existing branch (even though the PR is closed), or should they be committed to main, or should the PR be reopened after the changes are made?

For item 3, I need to clarify the prompt wording so it explicitly instructs both the final answer and GitHub comments to be in Korean, not just the comments.

For item 4, the core challenge is distinguishing between the two reviews since they both post as the GitHub Actions bot. The simplest approach is to have each review prefix its comments with a label—something like "### GPT-5.5 리뷰" or "### Claude Opus 4.8 리뷰"—so they're visually distinct even though they come from the same bot identity.

I should check the opencode action's source code to see if it supports custom comment headers or labeling, and I also want to look at the existing comments on PR #1 to understand the current format and what I'm working with. The most practical approach is to have each job include a distinct header in its comment body with the model name, so they're visually distinguishable even though both are authored by the same `github-actions[bot]` account. Let me check the action's inputs to see if there's a built-in way to customize comment formatting or if I need to work with the prompt directly. I'll fetch the action.yml from the anomalyco/opencode repository to understand what options are available, then gather the current PR comments to see the existing format. Fetching the action.yml from the pinned commit to see how the opencode action formats its comments, then running all the research queries in parallel — the issue comments, review comments, pull request reviews, current run status, and the action source to understand what markers or footers might distinguish between the two runs.

I'm in plan mode (read-only), so I'll research how to do each item and then present a plan. Let me gather the key facts: current run state, how the comments are currently posted on PR #1, and how the underlying opencode action handles comment identity.

---

state.output:
name: "opencode GitHub Action"
description: "Run opencode in GitHub Actions workflows"
branding:
  icon: "code"
  color: "orange"

inputs:
  model:
    description: "Model to use"
    required: true

  agent:
    description: "Agent to use. Must be a primary agent. Falls back to default_agent from config or 'build' if not found."
    required: false

  share:
    description: "Share the opencode session (defaults to true for public repos)"
    required: false

  prompt:
    description: "Custom prompt to override the default prompt"
    required: false

  use_github_token:
    description: "Use GITHUB_TOKEN directly instead of OpenCode App token exchange. When true, skips OIDC and uses the GITHUB_TOKEN env var."
    required: false
    default: "false"

  mentions:
    description: "Comma-separated list of trigger phrases (case-insensitive). Defaults to '/opencode,/oc'"
    required: false

  variant:
    description: "Model variant for provider-specific reasoning effort (e.g., high, max, minimal)"
    required: false

  oidc_base_url:
    description: "Base URL for OIDC token exchange API. Only required when running a custom GitHub App install. Defaults to https://api.opencode.ai"
    required: false

runs:
  using: "composite"
  steps:
    - name: Get opencode version
      id: version
      shell: bash
      run: |
        VERSION=$(curl -sf https://api.github.com/repos/anomalyco/opencode/releases/latest | grep -o '"tag_name": *"[^"]*"' | cut -d'"' -f4)
        echo "version=${VERSION:-latest}" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT

    - name: Cache opencode
      id: cache
      uses: actions/cache@v4
      with:
        path: ~/.opencode/bin
        key: opencode-${{ runner.os }}-${{ runner.arch }}-${{ steps.version.outputs.version }}

    - name: Install opencode
      if: steps.cache.outputs.cache-hit != 'true'
      shell: bash
      run: curl -fsSL https://opencode.ai/install | bash

    - name: Add opencode to PATH
      shell: bash
      run: echo "$HOME/.opencode/bin" >> $GITHUB_PATH

    - name: Run opencode
      shell: bash
      id: run_opencode
      run: opencode github run
      env:
        MODEL: ${{ inputs.model }}
        AGENT: ${{ inputs.agent }}
        SHARE: ${{ inputs.share }}
        PROMPT: ${{ inputs.prompt }}
        USE_GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ inputs.use_github_token }}
        MENTIONS: ${{ inputs.mentions }}
        VARIANT: ${{ inputs.variant }}
        OIDC_BASE_URL: ${{ inputs.oidc_base_url }}
state.title: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anomalyco/opencode/58a27b95c155d3f7d9b9f25b30eb1233bfb0eae5/github/action.yml (text/plain; charset=utf-8)

parts.text:
I'm reviewing the run status and realizing the workflow actually completed successfully—both reviews show `completed/success`—so the run the user wanted to stop may already be finished. But they specifically asked to stop a running action, so I need to reconsider whether there's a different active run I'm missing.

The reviews have posted and they're critiquing my fix itself. The GPT reviewer claims that `persist-credentials: true` is already the default for `actions/checkout@v6`, implying my change is redundant. But that's misleading in context—the original workflow explicitly set it to `false`, so my change from `false` back to `true` did matter and restored the default credential persistence that was being stripped.

I should verify whether v6 actually changed the default to `false` or if it's still `true`, but either way, the empirical evidence is clear: the run succeeded after my change, which strongly suggests the `false`→`true` fix was what resolved the git fetch auth failure. The reviewer might be evaluating the line in isolation rather than as a diff from the original `false` setting.

Looking at the broader feedback, the reviewer raised some valid points worth considering: the two jobs have significant code duplication, the `prompt` default in action.yml is never actually used since both callers override it, and there's the ongoing debate about the `persist-credentials` line itself. But for the immediate task at hand—stopping the action, closing the PR, and fixing the Korean prompts—those are secondary concerns. The key thing is that the reviews are posted under the bot account and the run succeeded.

To distinguish between the two reviews going forward, I could instruct each agent to prefix its comments with a model-specific header—something like "🟦 **GPT-5.5 리뷰**" for the GPT review and "🟪 **Claude Opus 4.8 리뷰**" for the Opus review. That way, even though both post as `github-actions[bot]`, readers can immediately see which model generated which feedback.

The action currently has no input for custom bot identity or comment labels, and using `use_github_token: true` locks everything to the `github-actions[bot]` identity. I could theoretically set up separate GitHub Apps for each reviewer to get distinct bot accounts, but that's heavy infrastructure overhead. The simpler path is the prompt-based header approach—it requires no new secrets or apps, just clear instructions to each model to label its own output.

I'm drafting the prompts now: each one will instruct the model to respond entirely in Korean (both the final answer and all GitHub comments) and prefix its comments with a distinct emoji header so readers can immediately see which reviewer wrote what. This solves both the language requirement and the distinguishability problem without extra setup.

For the workflow files themselves, I'm thinking through where the changes should live. Since the PR is being closed, committing to that branch afterward won't reopen it or retrigger the workflow—pushing to a closed PR's branch only fires events if the PR is open. So I could either commit the prompt updates to the existing branch and leave them there, push directly to main, or just let the user decide locally. The key is making sure the changes don't get lost and are ready whenever they want to use the workflow next.

I should also double-check whether there's actually a running action to stop—the last run completed successfully, so there may be nothing to cancel unless something new is queued. And I need to clarify with the user whether they want to close the PR and then open a fresh one to test the fixed prompts, or just close it and keep the changes staged for later. Let me verify there are no currently running actions by checking the run list, so I can accurately say whether cancellation is needed or if everything's already complete. I should also make sure the prompt instructions are explicit enough about Korean for the final answer—since the opencode action posts the assistant's final message as a PR comment, emphasizing that both the summary and all GitHub comments must be in Korean should cover it. The prompt-header approach for distinguishing between the two agents is low-risk and good enough. I'm thinking through the safest sequence: close the PR first to prevent any accidental re-triggers, then commit the prompt changes to the branch. If they want to re-test with the updated prompts, they can open a fresh PR from that branch. Let me verify there are no active runs still going, then I'll present the full plan with the key questions about where these changes should land and which distinguishing approach they prefer.

Let me confirm whether anything is still actually running before I plan the "stop" step.

---

parts.text:
The action has already completed successfully, so there's nothing to stop right now. PR #1 remains open, and I should note that closing it would prevent future runs from triggering, whereas stopping it now wouldn't have any effect since it's already finished.

For distinguishing between the two jobs, I've reconsidered the GitHub App approach — the OpenCode App token exchange uses the same bot identity for both, so that doesn't help. The realistic options are either prompt-based header labels (simple, no infrastructure) or separate GitHub Apps (heavy lift). I'll recommend the header label approach.

I should keep the focus on the four requests the user made rather than scope creeping into the reviewer's feedback about duplication. When updating both prompts with Korean support, I'll preserve the existing instructions about using the skill and having Opus read PR comments first, then add the Korean requirement and distinguishing header.

Now I'm thinking through where these changes should land — whether to close the PR and commit directly to the branch, or take a different approach. I should probably combine these into a single question with two parts rather than separate ones. And I need to note that stopping the action is already done—the real action is closing the PR to prevent re-triggers. On the reviewer's point about `persist-credentials`: they claimed it's a no-op since it defaults to true in checkout@v6, but that misses the fact that the original was explicitly false, so changing it to true is what fixed the issue. The run succeeded, which validates the fix regardless of whether the reviewer's claim about defaults is accurate. I'll mention this briefly as context but keep focus on the four main tasks ahead. I'm drafting the two review prompts with the key differences: both use the thermo-nuclear-code-quality-review skill and post in Korean, but GPT gets a 🟦 header and Opus gets a 🟪 header to distinguish them on GitHub. Now I need to ask the user two critical questions: where the updated prompts should land after closing PR #1 (commit to the existing branch, open a new PR for a fresh review run, or keep local-only), and how to make the two reviews distinguishable (prompt headers with both posting as github-actions[bot], or separate GitHub Apps entirely).

Here's what I found, then a plan and two decisions I need from you.

## Findings

**1. "Stop the running action" — already finished.** Run `27429484788` completed on its own: **both `gpt-review` and `opus-review` succeeded**, and there are no in-progress/queued runs. So there's nothing left to cancel. What actually keeps it from running again is closing the PR (a closed PR won't fire `synchronize`/`reopened`).

**2. The fix worked, and the reviews even posted (and critiqued it).** The review jobs left Korean comments on PR #1. Notably, the GPT review claims `persist-credentials: true` is "already the default for checkout@v6 so it doesn't fix anything." That's reviewing the line in isolation — the original was explicitly `false`, and empirically flipping `false→true` is what made the run succeed. (FYI only; not part of your asks.)

**3. Can the two reviews be distinguished today? No.** Every comment — the summary issue-comment, the inline comments, and the review — is authored by **`github-actions[bot]`** for *both* jobs. I checked the underlying action (`anomalyco/opencode/github`): its only inputs are `model, agent, share, prompt, use_github_token, mentions, variant, oidc_base_url`. There is **no input to set a comment label or a custom bot identity**, and `use_github_token: true` forces the `github-actions[bot]` author. So the only ways to tell them apart are (a) label inside the comment body via the prompt, or (b) give each job a different token identity (separate GitHub Apps).

**4. Final answers are currently in English.** The posted summary comments (e.g. opus's "Review complete. I loaded the skill…") are in English — confirming your ask to force Korean for the final answer too, not just inline comments.

## Proposed plan

1. **Close PR #1** (`gh pr close 1`), keeping the branch. Confirm no run is active (already true).
2. **Rewrite both prompts** in `.github/workflows/review.yml` to (a) force Korean for the final answer *and* all GitHub comments, and (b) prefix every comment/review with a distinct per-model header. Proposed:

   - **gpt-review:**
     > Use the `thermo-nuclear-code-quality-review` skill to review it. Always answer in Korean — write BOTH your final summary answer and every GitHub comment/review in Korean. Start every GitHub comment and review with the line `🟦 GPT-5.5 리뷰` so it can be told apart from other reviewers.
   - **opus-review:**
     > Use the `thermo-nuclear-code-quality-review` skill to review it. First read the existing PR comments using the gh CLI. Always answer in Korean — write BOTH your final summary answer and every GitHub comment/review in Korean. Start every GitHub comment and review with the line `🟪 Claude Opus 4.8 리뷰` so it can be told apart from other reviewers.

3. **Commit the prompt change** to wherever you choose below.

Two tradeoffs I want your call on before executing:

---

**Translation (Korean):**

제가 확인한 내용과 계획, 그리고 결정해 주셔야 할 두 가지를 정리했습니다.

## 확인 결과

**1. “실행 중인 액션 중지” — 이미 완료되었습니다.** 실행 `27429484788`은 자체적으로 완료되었습니다. **`gpt-review`와 `opus-review`가 모두 성공**했고, 진행 중이거나 대기 중인 실행은 없습니다. 따라서 취소할 것은 남아 있지 않습니다. 실제로 다시 실행되지 않게 막는 것은 PR을 닫는 것입니다. 닫힌 PR은 `synchronize`/`reopened`를 트리거하지 않습니다.

**2. 수정은 효과가 있었고, 리뷰도 게시되었습니다(그리고 그 수정에 대한 비판도 있었습니다).** 리뷰 작업들이 PR #1에 한국어 댓글을 남겼습니다. 특히 GPT 리뷰는 `persist-credentials: true`가 “checkout@v6의 기본값이므로 아무것도 고치지 않는다”고 주장했습니다. 이는 해당 줄만 따로 보고 리뷰한 것입니다. 원래는 명시적으로 `false`였고, 실제로 `false→true`로 바꾼 것이 실행 성공을 만든 원인입니다. (참고용일 뿐이며, 요청하신 사항의 일부는 아닙니다.)

**3. 현재 두 리뷰를 구분할 수 있나요? 아니요.** 요약 이슈 댓글, 인라인 댓글, 리뷰 등 모든 댓글의 작성자는 두 작업 모두 **`github-actions[bot]`**입니다. 내부 액션(`anomalyco/opencode/github`)을 확인했는데, 입력값은 `model, agent, share, prompt, use_github_token, mentions, variant, oidc_base_url`뿐입니다. **댓글 라벨이나 커스텀 봇 정체성을 설정하는 입력값은 없고**, `use_github_token: true`는 작성자를 `github-actions[bot]`으로 고정합니다. 따라서 구분할 수 있는 방법은 (a) 프롬프트를 통해 댓글 본문 안에 라벨을 넣거나, (b) 각 작업에 서로 다른 토큰 정체성(별도 GitHub Apps)을 부여하는 것뿐입니다.

**4. 최종 답변은 현재 영어입니다.** 게시된 요약 댓글(예: opus의 “Review complete. I loaded the skill…”)은 영어입니다. 이는 인라인 댓글뿐 아니라 최종 답변까지 한국어로 강제해야 한다는 요청을 확인해 줍니다.

## 제안하는 계획

1. **PR #1을 닫습니다**(`gh pr close 1`). 브랜치는 유지합니다. 활성 실행이 없는 것은 이미 확인했습니다.
2. `.github/workflows/review.yml`의 **두 프롬프트를 다시 작성**하여 (a) 최종 답변과 모든 GitHub 댓글을 한국어로 강제하고, (b) 모든 댓글/리뷰 앞에 모델별로 구분되는 헤더를 붙입니다. 제안안은 다음과 같습니다.

   - **gpt-review:**
     > `thermo-nuclear-code-quality-review` 스킬을 사용해 리뷰하세요. 항상 한국어로 답변하세요 — 최종 요약 답변과 모든 GitHub 댓글/리뷰를 모두 한국어로 작성하세요. 다른 리뷰어와 구분할 수 있도록 모든 GitHub 댓글과 리뷰를 `🟦 GPT-5.5 리뷰` 줄로 시작하세요.
   - **opus-review:**
     > `thermo-nuclear-code-quality-review` 스킬을 사용해 리뷰하세요. 먼저 gh CLI를 사용해 기존 PR 댓글을 읽으세요. 항상 한국어로 답변하세요 — 최종 요약 답변과 모든 GitHub 댓글/리뷰를 모두 한국어로 작성하세요. 다른 리뷰어와 구분할 수 있도록 모든 GitHub 댓글과 리뷰를 `🟪 Claude Opus 4.8 리뷰` 줄로 시작하세요.

3. **프롬프트 변경 사항을 커밋**합니다. 위치는 아래에서 선택해 주시면 됩니다.

실행하기 전에 결정해 주셨으면 하는 두 가지 트레이드오프가 있습니다.

parts.text:
The user's chosen approach means I need to pivot toward implementing separate GitHub Apps for each reviewer bot, which is a more complex solution than the initial plan. I'm now thinking through what this actually requires—creating two distinct apps, installing them on the repository, and managing their separate identities and permissions.

The core challenge is that GitHub Apps can only be created through the GitHub UI or a manifest flow, and the private keys need to be stored as secrets in the repository. Once set up, each workflow job would generate an installation access token for its respective app and pass that token to the composite action so comments appear under the correct bot identity. This is definitely feasible but requires careful manual setup on the user's end before any automation can take over. Completing the token setup...

The key insight is that the App installation token can be passed to the composite action for posting comments (which will appear as the bot), while git operations will still use the default GITHUB_TOKEN that was configured during checkout — so there's no conflict between the two token sources.

Actually, to keep things consistent and avoid any potential mismatches, the cleaner approach is to have the checkout step itself use the App token with `persist-credentials: true`, so the same token is used for both git operations and API calls. This requires the `create-github-app-token` action to run first, then checkout uses that token, and finally the composite action receives it as well. The App needs the right permissions (contents:read for git, plus write access for PRs and issues), and this design keeps everything aligned.

The main constraint here is that creating GitHub Apps is a manual browser-based process — I can't automate that part. So the user will need to create the two Apps themselves through the GitHub UI, set up the permissions, generate private keys, and install them on the repo. Then they'd provide me the App IDs and keys (or set the secrets themselves). I should be very explicit about this prerequisite and confirm they're comfortable with that overhead before proceeding with the full plan.

The workflow itself will need to be updated with the App token steps, and then we'd close the current PR and open a new one to trigger the re-test with the distinct bot identities posting in Korean. But that re-test only works if the GitHub Apps and secrets are already in place, so the manual setup has to happen first.

I should present the full plan with a clear split between what the user needs to do manually (create the two apps, install them, add the secrets) and what I'll handle (updating the workflow, closing/opening PRs). I'll propose sensible defaults for the app names like `hena-gpt-reviewer` and `hena-opus-reviewer` so the bot comments show up with those identities, but I should confirm with the user whether they want those names and whether they prefer org-level or personal app ownership.

I also need to verify the exact version and inputs for `actions/create-github-app-token` to pin it correctly in the plan—I'm checking whether v2 exists now and confirming the action uses the app installation token directly when passed as `GITHUB_TOKEN` so the bot identity shows up in the comments.

The key insight is that when the opencode action receives an app installation token as `GITHUB_TOKEN`, GitHub's API will attribute all actions (like posting comments) to the app's bot user. However, I'm now thinking through whether the permission check the action performs—verifying the triggering actor's repo access—will still work with an app token, since that endpoint requires the caller to have certain permissions like push access or administration read, and I need to map what an app token with Contents and Pull Requests write permissions actually grants.

This is a real risk: the action's permission assertion step might fail or behave differently with an app token than with the default token, so I should validate this during testing and potentially grant the app additional permissions like Members or Administration read if needed. Before finalizing the plan, I should verify the latest version and inputs for the token-creation action, confirm whether the org allows creating apps, and check if there are any existing secrets or apps already configured.

Good — that changes the shape of the work. The GitHub App approach is the robust way to get distinct comment authors, but it has a hard manual prerequisite (Apps can't be created via CLI). Let me research the exact wiring so the plan is accurate.

---

**Translation (Korean):**

좋습니다 — 그러면 작업의 형태가 달라집니다. GitHub App 방식은 서로 다른 댓글 작성자를 확보하는 견고한 방법이지만, 반드시 수동으로 해야 하는 전제 조건이 있습니다(App은 CLI로 생성할 수 없습니다). 계획이 정확하도록 구체적인 연결 방식을 조사해 보겠습니다.

state.title: Check org status, role, secrets, and existing app installations

state.output:
# Create GitHub App Token

[![test](https://github.com/actions/create-github-app-token/actions/workflows/test.yml/badge.svg)](https://github.com/actions/create-github-app-token/actions/workflows/test.yml)

GitHub Action for creating a GitHub App installation access token.

## Usage

In order to use this action, you need to:

1. [Register new GitHub App](https://docs.github.com/apps/creating-github-apps/setting-up-a-github-app/creating-a-github-app).
2. [Store the App's Client ID in your repository variables](https://docs.github.com/actions/how-tos/write-workflows/choose-what-workflows-do/use-variables#defining-configuration-variables-for-multiple-workflows) (example: `APP_CLIENT_ID`).
3. [Store the App's private key in your repository secrets](https://docs.github.com/actions/how-tos/write-workflows/choose-what-workflows-do/use-secrets?tool=webui#creating-secrets-for-a-repository) (example: `APP_PRIVATE_KEY`).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> An installation access token expires after 1 hour. Please [see this comment](https://github.com/actions/create-github-app-token/issues/121#issuecomment-2043214796) for alternative approaches if you have long-running processes.

### Create a token for the current repository

```yaml
name: Run tests on staging
on:
  push:
    branches:
      - main

jobs:
  hello-world:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/create-github-app-token@v3
        id: app-token
        with:
          client-id: ${{ vars.APP_CLIENT_ID }}
          private-key: ${{ secrets.APP_PRIVATE_KEY }}
      - uses: ./actions/staging-tests
        with:
          token: ${{ steps.app-token.outputs.token }}
```

### Use app token with `actions/checkout`

```yaml
on: [pull_request]

jobs:
  auto-format:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/create-github-app-token@v3
        id: app-token
        with:
          # required
          client-id: ${{ vars.APP_CLIENT_ID }}
          private-key: ${{ secrets.APP_PRIVATE_KEY }}
      - uses: actions/checkout@v6
        with:
          token: ${{ steps.app-token.outputs.token }}
          ref: ${{ github.head_ref }}
          # Make sure the value of GITHUB_TOKEN will not be persisted in repo's config
          persist-credentials: false
      - uses: creyD/prettier_action@v6
        with:
          github_token: ${{ steps.app-token.outputs.token }}
```

### Create a Git committer string for an app installation

```yaml
on: [pull_request]

jobs:
  auto-format:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/create-github-app-token@v3
        id: app-token
        with:
          # required
          client-id: ${{ vars.APP_CLIENT_ID }}
          private-key: ${{ secrets.APP_PRIVATE_KEY }}
      - name: Get GitHub App User ID
        id: get-user-id
        run: echo "user-id=$(gh api "/users/${{ steps.app-token.outputs.app-slug }}[bot]" --jq .id)" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
        env:
          GH_TOKEN: ${{ steps.app-token.outputs.token }}
      - id: committer
        run: echo "string=${{ steps.app-token.outputs.app-slug }}[bot] <${{ steps.get-user-id.outputs.user-id }}+${{ steps.app-token.outputs.app-slug }}[bot]@users.noreply.github.com>"  >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
      - run: echo "committer string is ${{ steps.committer.outputs.string }}"
```

### Configure Git CLI for an app's bot user

```yaml
on: [pull_request]

jobs:
  auto-format:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/create-github-app-token@v3
        id: app-token
        with:
          # required
          client-id: ${{ vars.APP_CLIENT_ID }}
          private-key: ${{ secrets.APP_PRIVATE_KEY }}
          permission-contents: write
      - name: Get GitHub App User ID
        id: get-user-id
        run: echo "user-id=$(gh api "/users/${{ steps.app-token.outputs.app-slug }}[bot]" --jq .id)" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
        env:
          GH_TOKEN: ${{ steps.app-token.outputs.token }}
      - run: |
          git config --global user.name '${{ steps.app-token.outputs.app-slug }}[bot]'
          git config --global user.email '${{ steps.get-user-id.outputs.user-id }}+${{ steps.app-token.outputs.app-slug }}[bot]@users.noreply.github.com'
          gh auth setup-git
        env:
          GH_TOKEN: ${{ steps.app-token.outputs.token }}
      # Git commands like commit and push work using the bot user
      - run: |
          git add .
          git commit -m "Auto-generated changes"
          git push
```

> [!TIP]
> The `<BOT USER ID>` is the numeric user ID of the app's bot user, which can be found under `https://api.github.com/users/<app-slug>%5Bbot%5D`.
>
> For example, we can check at `https://api.github.com/users/dependabot[bot]` to see the user ID of Dependabot is 49699333.
>
> Alternatively, you can use the [octokit/request-action](https://github.com/octokit/request-action) to get the ID.

### Create a token for all repositories in the current owner's installation

```yaml
on: [workflow_dispatch]

jobs:
  hello-world:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/create-github-app-token@v3
        id: app-token
        with:
          client-id: ${{ vars.APP_CLIENT_ID }}
          private-key: ${{ secrets.APP_PRIVATE_KEY }}
          owner: ${{ github.repository_owner }}
      - uses: peter-evans/create-or-update-comment@v4
        with:
          token: ${{ steps.app-token.outputs.token }}
          issue-number: ${{ github.event.issue.number }}
          body: "Hello, World!"
```

### Create a token for multiple repositories in the current owner's installation

```yaml
on: [issues]

jobs:
  hello-world:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/create-github-app-token@v3
        id: app-token
        with:
          client-id: ${{ vars.APP_CLIENT_ID }}
          private-key: ${{ secrets.APP_PRIVATE_KEY }}
          owner: ${{ github.repository_owner }}
          repositories: |
            repo1
            repo2
      - uses: peter-evans/create-or-update-comment@v4
        with:
          token: ${{ steps.app-token.outputs.token }}
          issue-number: ${{ github.event.issue.number }}
          body: "Hello, World!"
```

You can include the current repository in the list with `${{ github.repository }}`:

```yaml
repositories: ${{ github.repository }},repo2
```

### Create a token for all repositories in another owner's installation

```yaml
on: [issues]

jobs:
  hello-world:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/create-github-app-token@v3
        id: app-token
        with:
          client-id: ${{ vars.APP_CLIENT_ID }}
          private-key: ${{ secrets.APP_PRIVATE_KEY }}
          owner: another-owner
      - uses: peter-evans/create-or-update-comment@v4
        with:
          token: ${{ steps.app-token.outputs.token }}
          issue-number: ${{ github.event.issue.number }}
          body: "Hello, World!"
```

### Create a token for an enterprise installation

```yaml
on: [workflow_dispatch]

jobs:
  hello-world:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/create-github-app-token@v3
        id: app-token
        with:
          client-id: ${{ vars.APP_CLIENT_ID }}
          private-key: ${{ secrets.APP_PRIVATE_KEY }}
          enterprise: my-enterprise-slug
      - name: Call enterprise management REST API with gh
        run: |
          gh api /enterprises/my-enterprise-slug/apps/installable_organizations
        env:
          GH_TOKEN: ${{ steps.app-token.outputs.token }}
```

### Create a token with specific permissions

> [!NOTE]
> Selected permissions must be granted to the specified app installation. Setting a permission that the installation does not have will result in an error.

```yaml
on: [issues]

jobs:
  hello-world:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/create-github-app-token@v3
        id: app-token
        with:
          client-id: ${{ vars.APP_CLIENT_ID }}
          private-key: ${{ secrets.APP_PRIVATE_KEY }}
          owner: ${{ github.repository_owner }}
          permission-issues: write
      - uses: peter-evans/create-or-update-comment@v4
        with:
          token: ${{ steps.app-token.outputs.token }}
          issue-number: ${{ github.event.issue.number }}
          body: "Hello, World!"
```

### Create tokens for multiple user or organization accounts

You can use a matrix strategy to create tokens for multiple user or organization accounts.

> [!NOTE]
> See [this documentation](https://docs.github.com/actions/using-workflows/workflow-commands-for-github-actions#multiline-strings) for information on using multiline strings in workflows.

```yaml
on: [workflow_dispatch]

jobs:
  set-matrix:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    outputs:
      matrix: ${{ steps.set.outputs.matrix }}
    steps:
      - id: set
        run: echo 'matrix=[{"owner":"owner1"},{"owner":"owner2","repos":["repo1"]}]' >>"$GITHUB_OUTPUT"

  use-matrix:
    name: "@${{ matrix.owners-and-repos.owner }} installation"
    needs: [set-matrix]
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    strategy:
      matrix:
        owners-and-repos: ${{ fromJson(needs.set-matrix.outputs.matrix) }}

    steps:
      - uses: actions/create-github-app-token@v3
        id: app-token
        with:
          client-id: ${{ vars.APP_CLIENT_ID }}
          private-key: ${{ secrets.APP_PRIVATE_KEY }}
          owner: ${{ matrix.owners-and-repos.owner }}
          repositories: ${{ join(matrix.owners-and-repos.repos) }}
      - uses: octokit/request-action@v2.x
        id: get-installation-repositories
        with:
          route: GET /installation/repositories
        env:
          GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ steps.app-token.outputs.token }}
      - run: echo "$MULTILINE_JSON_STRING"
        env:
          MULTILINE_JSON_STRING: ${{ steps.get-installation-repositories.outputs.data }}
```

### Run the workflow in a github.com repository against an organization in GitHub Enterprise Server

```yaml
on: [push]

jobs:
  create_issue:
    runs-on: self-hosted

    steps:
      - name: Create GitHub App token
        id: create_token
        uses: actions/create-github-app-token@v3
        with:
          client-id: ${{ vars.GHES_APP_CLIENT_ID }}
          private-key: ${{ secrets.GHES_APP_PRIVATE_KEY }}
          owner: ${{ vars.GHES_INSTALLATION_ORG }}
          github-api-url: ${{ vars.GITHUB_API_URL }}

      - name: Create issue
        uses: octokit/request-action@v2.x
        with:
          route: POST /repos/${{ github.repository }}/issues
          title: "New issue from workflow"
          body: "This is a new issue created from a GitHub Action workflow."
        env:
          GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ steps.create_token.outputs.token }}
```

### Proxy support

This action relies on Node.js native proxy support.

If you set `HTTP_PROXY` or `HTTPS_PROXY`, also set `NODE_USE_ENV_PROXY: "1"` on the action step so Node.js honors those variables. If you need proxy bypass rules, set `NO_PROXY` alongside them.

```yaml
- uses: actions/create-github-app-token@v3
  id: app-token
  env:
    HTTPS_PROXY: http://proxy.example.com:8080
    NO_PROXY: github.example.com
    NODE_USE_ENV_PROXY: "1"
  with:
    client-id: ${{ vars.APP_CLIENT_ID }}
    private-key: ${{ secrets.APP_PRIVATE_KEY }}
```

## Inputs

### `client-id` or `app-id`

**Required:** GitHub App Client ID.

> [!NOTE]
> The legacy `app-id` input is also accepted, but `client-id` is recommended.

### `private-key`

**Required:** GitHub App private key. Escaped newlines (`\\n`) will be automatically replaced with actual newlines.

Some other actions may require the private key to be Base64 encoded. To avoid recreating a new secret, it can be decoded on the fly, but it needs to be managed securely. Here is an example of how this can be achieved:

```yaml
steps:
  - name: Decode the GitHub App Private Key
    id: decode
    run: |
      private_key=$(echo "${{ secrets.APP_PRIVATE_KEY }}" | base64 -d | awk 'BEGIN {ORS="\\n"} {print}' | head -c -2) &> /dev/null
      echo "::add-mask::$private_key"
      echo "private-key=$private_key" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
  - name: Generate GitHub App Token
    id: app-token
    uses: actions/create-github-app-token@v3
    with:
      client-id: ${{ vars.APP_CLIENT_ID }}
      private-key: ${{ steps.decode.outputs.private-key }}
```

### `owner`

**Optional:** The owner of the GitHub App installation. If empty, defaults to the current repository owner.

### `repositories`

**Optional:** Comma or newline-separated list of repositories to grant access to.

> [!NOTE]
> If `owner` is set and `repositories` is empty, access will be scoped to all repositories in the provided repository owner's installation. If `owner` and `repositories` are empty, access will be scoped to only the current repository.
>
> Repository entries may include an owner, for example `owner/repo1`. The owner portion must match the `owner` input, or the current repository owner if `owner` is unset.

### `enterprise`

**Optional:** The slug of the enterprise account to generate a token for an enterprise installation.

> [!NOTE]
> The `enterprise` input is mutually exclusive with `owner` and `repositories`. Use it when the GitHub App is installed on an enterprise account. Enterprise installation tokens can call enterprise APIs, but do not grant organization or repository access.

### `permission-<permission name>`

**Optional:** The permissions to grant to the token. By default, the token inherits all of the installation's permissions. We recommend to explicitly list the permissions that are required for a use case. This follows GitHub's own recommendation to [control permissions of `GITHUB_TOKEN` in workflows](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/writing-workflows/choosing-what-your-workflow-does/controlling-permissions-for-github_token). The documentation also lists all available permissions, just prefix the permission key with `permission-` (e.g., `pull-requests` → `permission-pull-requests`).

The reason we define one `permision-<permission name>` input per permission is to benefit from type intelligence and input validation built into GitHub's action runner.

### `skip-token-revoke`

**Optional:** If true, the token will not be revoked when the current job is complete.

### `github-api-url`

**Optional:** The URL of the GitHub REST API. Defaults to the URL of the GitHub Rest API where the workflow is run from.

## Outputs

### `token`

GitHub App installation access token.

### `installation-id`

GitHub App installation ID.

### `app-slug`

GitHub App slug.

## How it works

The action creates an installation access token using [the `POST /app/installations/{installation_id}/access_tokens` endpoint](https://docs.github.com/rest/apps/apps?apiVersion=2022-11-28#create-an-installation-access-token-for-an-app).

The token target depends on the inputs: `enterprise` creates a token for an enterprise installation, `owner` without `repositories` creates a token for all repositories in the owner's installation, `repositories` scopes the token to those repositories, and no target inputs scopes the token to the current repository.

1. The token inherits all the installation's permissions.
2. The token is set as output `token` which can be used in subsequent steps.
3. Unless the `skip-token-revoke` input is set to true, the token is revoked in the `post` step of the action, which means it cannot be passed to another job.
4. The token is masked, it cannot be logged accidentally.

> [!NOTE]
> Installation permissions can differ from the app's permissions they belong to. Installation permissions are set when an app is installed on an account. When the app adds more permissions after the installation, an account administrator will have to approve the new permissions before they are set on the installation.

## Contributing

[CONTRIBUTING.md](CONTRIBUTING.md)

## License

[MIT](LICENSE)
state.title: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/actions/create-github-app-token/main/README.md (text/plain; charset=utf-8)

parts.text:
I've confirmed the key details: `hena-agent` is a free-tier organization with hena-dev as an admin, giving them the ability to create and manage organization-owned GitHub Apps. The only secret configured is `OPENCODE_AUTH_JSON`, and there are no visible app installations currently set up.

For the workflow implementation, I'll use `actions/create-github-app-token@v3`, which takes a client-id and private-key to generate a scoped token that expires in an hour—perfect for a single review run. I'm planning to store the client-ids as repository variables and the private keys as secrets following the action's recommended pattern, with separate credentials for each reviewer app. I'll pin the action to a specific v3 commit SHA to match the repo's pinning philosophy, resolving the exact SHA when I execute the setup.

For each job, I'm minting the app token first, then using it in the checkout step so git operations use the app's credentials, and finally passing it to the opencode review action so comments post as the app bot. The same pattern applies for both the GPT and Opus reviewers with their respective secrets and prompts.

Now I'm hitting a permission issue — the opencode action asserts the user's permissions by calling the collaborators endpoint, which requires push access to the repo. For a GitHub App token, I need to figure out what permission grants access to read collaborator permissions. The docs suggest it might need `Administration: read`, but that's a powerful permission to grant. I'm realizing the safest approach might be to not restrict the token permissions at all and let it inherit all the installation's permissions by default. The token will automatically inherit all the app's installation permissions, so I don't need to explicitly set permission flags—I can just rely on what's granted at install time. For the manual setup, I need to create each app under the org with the right repository permissions (Contents read, Issues and Pull requests read-write), generate a private key, install it scoped to the hena repo, and then add the Client ID and private key to the repo as a variable and secret.

I can automate step 5 if the user provides me the Client IDs and .pem files, but steps 1–4 require browser access. Once I have those values, I'll update the workflow with the new prompts and app-token steps, close the current PR, commit to a fresh branch, and open a new PR to verify the bots are posting as distinct authors and the output is in Korean. The bot's display name will be derived from the app name (lowercased with hyphens), so I should let the user choose the names—though I'll suggest defaults—and note that GitHub App names must be globally unique.

Since the user specifically chose separate GitHub Apps over header labels for distinction, I'll keep the prompts clean and focused on the Korean instruction without mandatory headers, though I can mention that adding a header is optional.

Now I'm thinking through the workflow sequencing: the two bot identities will post comments under their respective app accounts once the workflow runs, but there's a dependency issue—the workflow will fail at the token creation step if the GitHub Apps and their secrets aren't configured first. So the manual setup absolutely has to happen before opening the test PR, otherwise the run will break immediately. I should gate the PR on that being complete.

I'm also reconsidering the `id-token: write` permission—it was needed for the OIDC token exchange path, but since we're using `use_github_token: true` instead, that permission is actually unnecessary now.

There's another angle worth exploring: instead of creating two separate apps, we could use one custom app plus the default `github-actions[bot]` to get two distinct identities with half the setup work. It's a bit asymmetric visually, but it still gives the user what they wanted—separate authors for the two reviews. I should present this as an option alongside the two-app approach and let them choose based on their preference for clarity versus setup simplicity.

Before I finalize the plan, I need to confirm a few execution details with them: the specific bot names (which must be globally unique and user-facing), who'll handle setting the four config values—whether they'll do it through the GitHub UI or hand me the credentials so I can use the CLI—and whether the one-app shortcut appeals to them. These are concrete confirmations I can bundle into one question rather than reopening the bigger architectural decisions we've already settled. I'm checking the token lifecycle — the app token from `create-github-app-token` is valid for an hour and gets revoked in the post-step, which is fine for a few-minute review run. The token is scoped to the current repo with the installation's permissions, and the app has the necessary Issues:write and Pull requests:write permissions for adding reactions. Since we're closing PR #1 anyway, the noise comments there won't matter. For the re-test, I'll use a fresh branch name (like `chore/opencode-ci-review-v2`) to avoid GitHub potentially reopening the closed PR instead of creating a new one. I'm settling on two key questions to confirm before finalizing the plan. First, I need to nail down the app names and identities — whether we're going with two separate bots or using the single-app shortcut. Second, I need to clarify who provisions the credentials: whether you'll set them up in the GitHub UI or hand me the Client IDs and .pem files so I can configure them via the gh CLI. I'll present the full plan first with those two questions, and I'll make sure to note the actor-permission risk and that we'll validate it during the re-test.

Research done. `hena-agent` is an **Organization** and `hena-dev` is an **org admin**, so you can create org-owned Apps and install them. `actions/create-github-app-token@v3` is the right tool to mint per-app tokens in the workflow. Here's the full plan.

## What "separate GitHub Apps" requires

To get distinct comment authors (e.g. `hena-gpt-reviewer[bot]` vs `hena-opus-reviewer[bot]`), each job must post with a different **GitHub App installation token**. The pinned opencode action already posts comments using whatever token we hand it via `github-token` + `use_github_token: true` — so passing an App installation token makes its comments appear under that App's bot. No change to the pinned action is needed.

**Hard prerequisite (manual, browser-only — cannot be done by CLI):** GitHub Apps can't be created with `gh`. You (as org admin) must create + install two Apps. Everything after that I can automate.

## Phase A — you do this in the browser (per App, ×2)

For **Hena GPT Reviewer** and **Hena Opus Reviewer** (names must be globally unique on GitHub; the bot author = name slug + `[bot]`):
1. hena-agent org → Settings → Developer settings → GitHub Apps → **New GitHub App**.
2. Webhook: **uncheck Active**. Installable: **Only on this account**.
3. Repository permissions: **Contents: Read**, **Issues: Read & write**, **Pull requests: Read & write** (Metadata: Read is automatic).
4. Create → copy the **Client ID** → **Generate a private key** (downloads a `.pem`).
5. **Install** the App → scope to **only the `hena` repo**.

Then hand me the two Client IDs + two `.pem` files (or set them yourself).

## Phase B — I automate (execution)

1. Set repo config: vars `GPT_REVIEWER_CLIENT_ID`, `OPUS_REVIEWER_CLIENT_ID`; secrets `GPT_REVIEWER_PRIVATE_KEY`, `OPUS_REVIEWER_PRIVATE_KEY`.
2. **Close PR #1** (keep branch; nothing is currently running so there's nothing to cancel).
3. Rewrite `.github/workflows/review.yml`. Each job gains an app-token step and uses it for both checkout auth and commenting. Per job (gpt shown; opus mirrors with OPUS_* + opus prompt):

```yaml
  gpt-review:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions: { contents: read, pull-requests: write, issues: write }
    steps:
      - name: Mint GPT reviewer app token
        id: app-token
        uses: actions/create-github-app-token@v3   # pinned to a SHA at execution
        with:
          client-id: ${{ vars.GPT_REVIEWER_CLIENT_ID }}
          private-key: ${{ secrets.GPT_REVIEWER_PRIVATE_KEY }}
      - uses: actions/checkout@v6
        with: { fetch-depth: 1, persist-credentials: true, token: ${{ steps.app-token.outputs.token }} }
      - name: Review with GPT-5.5 (variant xhigh)
        uses: ./.github/actions/run-opencode-review
        with:
          model: openai/gpt-5.5
          variant: xhigh
          opencode-auth-json: ${{ secrets.OPENCODE_AUTH_JSON }}
          github-token: ${{ steps.app-token.outputs.token }}   # <- was secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN
          prompt: |
            Use the `thermo-nuclear-code-quality-review` skill to review it. Always answer in Korean — write BOTH your final summary answer and every comment/review you post on GitHub in Korean.
```

   The opus prompt adds "First read the existing PR comments using the gh CLI." The `needs: gpt-review` sequencing is unchanged.
4. Commit to a **new branch** `chore/opencode-ci-review-apps` (off the current branch so the 5 files are present) and **open PR #2** to re-test.
5. Watch the run and verify: both jobs succeed, comments post under the **two distinct bot identities**, and both the final summary and inline comments are in **Korean**.

## Risks I'll validate in the re-test
- **Actor-permission assertion:** the opencode action calls "Asserting permissions for user hena-dev". With an App token this *may* need a permission beyond the three above (e.g. it can't read collaborator permission). If it fails, the fix is to grant the App one more permission and re-run — I'll confirm during PR #2.
- Re-test PR #2 will trigger a fresh paid review run (your earlier choice).

A couple of confirmations before I (later) execute:

---

**Translation (Korean):**

조사 완료. `hena-agent`는 **Organization**이고 `hena-dev`는 **org admin**이므로, org 소유 App을 만들고 설치할 수 있습니다. 워크플로에서 App별 토큰을 발급하는 데에는 `actions/create-github-app-token@v3`가 적절한 도구입니다. 전체 계획은 아래와 같습니다.

## “별도의 GitHub Apps”에 필요한 것

서로 다른 댓글 작성자(예: `hena-gpt-reviewer[bot]` vs `hena-opus-reviewer[bot]`)를 얻으려면, 각 job이 서로 다른 **GitHub App installation token**으로 게시해야 합니다. 고정된 opencode action은 이미 `github-token` + `use_github_token: true`를 통해 전달한 토큰으로 댓글을 게시합니다. 따라서 App installation token을 전달하면 해당 App의 bot으로 댓글이 표시됩니다. 고정된 action은 변경할 필요가 없습니다.

**필수 선행 조건(수동, 브라우저에서만 가능 — CLI로는 불가):** GitHub Apps는 `gh`로 만들 수 없습니다. 당신이 org admin으로서 두 개의 App을 생성하고 설치해야 합니다. 그 이후는 제가 모두 자동화할 수 있습니다.

## Phase A — 브라우저에서 직접 수행(각 App별, ×2)

**Hena GPT Reviewer**와 **Hena Opus Reviewer**에 대해 진행합니다. 이름은 GitHub 전체에서 고유해야 하며, bot 작성자명은 이름 slug + `[bot]`입니다.
1. hena-agent org → Settings → Developer settings → GitHub Apps → **New GitHub App**.
2. Webhook: **Active 체크 해제**. Installable: **Only on this account**.
3. Repository permissions: **Contents: Read**, **Issues: Read & write**, **Pull requests: Read & write**(Metadata: Read는 자동).
4. 생성 → **Client ID** 복사 → **Generate a private key** 실행(`.pem` 다운로드).
5. App **Install** → 범위를 **`hena` repo만**으로 제한.

그런 다음 두 개의 Client ID와 두 개의 `.pem` 파일을 제게 전달해 주세요(또는 직접 설정하셔도 됩니다).

## Phase B — 제가 자동화(실행)

1. repo 설정: vars `GPT_REVIEWER_CLIENT_ID`, `OPUS_REVIEWER_CLIENT_ID`; secrets `GPT_REVIEWER_PRIVATE_KEY`, `OPUS_REVIEWER_PRIVATE_KEY`.
2. **PR #1 닫기**(branch는 유지; 현재 실행 중인 것은 없으므로 취소할 것도 없습니다).
3. `.github/workflows/review.yml` 재작성. 각 job에 app-token step을 추가하고 checkout 인증과 댓글 작성 모두에 해당 토큰을 사용합니다. job별 예시(gpt 표시; opus는 OPUS_* + opus prompt로 동일):

```yaml
  gpt-review:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions: { contents: read, pull-requests: write, issues: write }
    steps:
      - name: Mint GPT reviewer app token
        id: app-token
        uses: actions/create-github-app-token@v3   # 실행 시 SHA로 고정
        with:
          client-id: ${{ vars.GPT_REVIEWER_CLIENT_ID }}
          private-key: ${{ secrets.GPT_REVIEWER_PRIVATE_KEY }}
      - uses: actions/checkout@v6
        with: { fetch-depth: 1, persist-credentials: true, token: ${{ steps.app-token.outputs.token }} }
      - name: Review with GPT-5.5 (variant xhigh)
        uses: ./.github/actions/run-opencode-review
        with:
          model: openai/gpt-5.5
          variant: xhigh
          opencode-auth-json: ${{ secrets.OPENCODE_AUTH_JSON }}
          github-token: ${{ steps.app-token.outputs.token }}   # <- 기존 secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN
          prompt: |
            `thermo-nuclear-code-quality-review` skill을 사용해 리뷰하세요. 항상 한국어로 답변하세요 — GitHub에 게시하는 최종 요약 답변과 모든 댓글/리뷰를 모두 한국어로 작성하세요.
```

   opus prompt에는 “먼저 gh CLI를 사용해 기존 PR 댓글을 읽으세요.”가 추가됩니다. `needs: gpt-review` 순서는 그대로 유지됩니다.
4. 현재 branch에서 새 branch `chore/opencode-ci-review-apps`를 생성해 commit합니다(5개 파일이 존재하도록 현재 branch 기준). 그리고 재테스트를 위해 **PR #2**를 엽니다.
5. 실행을 지켜보고 검증합니다: 두 job이 모두 성공하고, 댓글이 **서로 다른 두 bot identity**로 게시되며, 최종 요약과 inline comments가 모두 **한국어**인지 확인합니다.

## 재테스트에서 검증할 리스크
- **Actor-permission assertion:** opencode action이 “Asserting permissions for user hena-dev”를 호출합니다. App token을 사용하면 위 세 가지 권한 외에 추가 권한이 필요할 수도 있습니다(예: collaborator permission을 읽지 못할 수 있음). 실패하면 App에 권한을 하나 더 부여하고 재실행하면 됩니다. PR #2에서 확인하겠습니다.
- 재테스트 PR #2는 새로운 유료 리뷰 실행을 트리거합니다(이전에 선택하신 사항).

제가 나중에 실행하기 전에 몇 가지 확인이 필요합니다:

questions.question: 두 GitHub 앱의 이름은 무엇으로 지정해야 하나요? 이 이름은 댓글 작성자(이름 슬러그 + [bot])가 되며, GitHub 전체에서 고유해야 합니다.

options.label: Hena GPT 리뷰어 / Hena Opus 리뷰어
options.description: 봇은 hena-gpt-reviewer[bot] 및 hena-opus-reviewer[bot]로 표시됩니다. GitHub에서 이름이 이미 사용 중인 경우 조정해야 합니다(예: 접미사 추가).

options.label: 하나의 앱 + 기본 봇 사용(2개 대신 앱 1개)
options.description: 더 가벼운 설정: 앱을 하나만 생성합니다(예: Opus용). Opus 댓글은 해당 이름의 봇으로 게시되고, GPT는 계속 github-actions[bot]으로 게시됩니다. 두 명의 서로 다른 작성자, 수작업은 절반이지만 이름 지정은 비대칭입니다.

questions.question: 앱이 생성되면 4개의 구성 값(변수로 설정되는 클라이언트 ID 2개 + 시크릿으로 설정되는 개인 키 2개)은 누가 설정하나요?

options.label: 나한테 넘겨줘. 내가 gh로 설정할게.
options.description: 두 개의 클라이언트 ID와 두 개의 .pem 개인 키 파일을 저에게 주시면, 제가 hena-agent/hena에서 gh variable set / gh secret set을 실행하겠습니다.

options.label: UI에서 변수/비밀값은 제가 직접 설정하겠습니다.
options.description: 리포지토리 변수와 시크릿은 직접 추가하시고, 저는 워크플로가 해당 이름으로 참조하도록 연결만 해드립니다.

state.title: Asked 2 questions

parts.text:
The user has already set up two GitHub Apps with their own credentials and private keys for both the GPT and Claude reviewers.

Now I'm facing a critical decision: I'm still in plan mode (the system reminder hasn't been explicitly rescinded), which means I shouldn't execute commands. But the user has just provided sensitive credentials, seemingly expecting me to proceed with setting the config values. I need to clarify whether they're approving execution or if they want me to stay in planning mode.

More importantly, I'm noticing a significant security concern that I need to surface. Looking back at this entire session holistically — installing third-party skills, disabling safety prompts, uploading live OAuth credentials to GitHub secrets, and now setting up GitHub Apps with write permissions — there's a pattern here that feels risky and potentially violates terms of service. I have a professional responsibility to flag this before proceeding further, regardless of what mode I'm in.

That said, stepping back: the user owns their own repo, credentials, and subscription, and they've been making deliberate choices at each step. The credentials aren't being exfiltrated to third parties — they're going into the user's own org. So this is a legitimate (if security-relaxed) personal setup, and my role is to flag risks and execute accurately.

But there's a critical constraint I need to respect: I'm in plan mode, which means I cannot make state-changing operations like setting secrets, editing files, or closing PRs. Those are non-readonly actions that violate the explicit plan-mode restriction.

What I should do instead is finalize the complete plan with the credentials and app names the user provided, then ask them to approve and exit plan mode before I proceed with execution. ## 최종 계획 — 실행 준비 완료(승인 대기 중)

저는 아직 **계획 모드(읽기 전용)**에 있으므로 아무것도 변경하지 않았습니다. 아래의 모든 단계는 상태를 변경하는 작업이며, 귀하의 승인 없이는 진행하지 않습니다. 실행 시 `.pem` 파일은 내용을 출력하지 않고 곧바로 `gh secret set`으로 파이프 처리하겠습니다.

- secret `OPUS_REVIEWER_PRIVATE_KEY` ← `~/Downloads/hena-claude-reviewer.2026-06-12.private-key.pem`

**2. PR #1 닫기** (`gh pr close 1`, 브랜치는 유지; 취소할 실행 중인 작업은 없음).

**3. `.github/workflows/review.yml` 다시 작성** — 작업별로: 해당 작업의 client-id/private-key를 사용해 `actions/create-github-app-token@v3`(SHA로 고정) 추가; `token: ${{ steps.app-token.outputs.token }}`로 `actions/checkout`; 해당 토큰을 composite action에 `github-token`으로 전달; 새로운 한국어 프롬프트(최종 답변 **및** GitHub 댓글을 한국어로). `needs: gpt-review` 순서는 유지.

**4. 새 브랜치 `chore/opencode-ci-review-apps`에 커밋**(현재 브랜치에서 분기하여 5개 파일이 diff에 포함되도록 함), 푸시 후 재테스트를 위해 **PR #2 열기**.

**5. PR #2의 실행 확인** 및 검증: 두 작업 모두 성공, 댓글이 `hena-gpt-reviewer[bot]` 및 `hena-claude-reviewer[bot]` 명의로 게시되고, 출력이 한국어인지 확인. App 토큰에서 opencode action의 "Asserting permissions" 단계가 실패하면 보고드리고 App 권한을 하나 더 부여합니다(앞서 언급한 유일한 위험).

이미 수락하신 결과에 대한 알림: PR #2는 새로운 유료 리뷰 실행을 트리거하며, App private key(이 repo에서 쓰기 권한 보유)는 repo secret에 저장됩니다.

승인하시면 1단계부터 실행하겠습니다. 원하시면 실제 App 이름과 맞추기 위해 두 번째 secret/var 이름을 `CLAUDE_REVIEWER_*`로 바꾸라고 말씀해 주세요.

diffs.patch:
Index: .github/workflows/review.yml
===================================================================
--- .github/workflows/review.yml	
+++ .github/workflows/review.yml	
@@ -1,64 +1,75 @@
 name: review
 
 on:
   pull_request:
     types: [opened, synchronize, reopened, ready_for_review]
 
 # OPENCODE_AUTH_JSON is a repo secret and is not exposed to PRs from forks.
 # These workflows are intended for same-repo PRs only.
+#
+# Each job mints its own GitHub App installation token so review comments post
+# under a distinct bot identity (hena-gpt-reviewer[bot] / hena-claude-reviewer[bot]).
+# The app token is also used for checkout so the opencode action's own
+# `git fetch origin` is authenticated.
 jobs:
   gpt-review:
     runs-on: ubuntu-latest
     permissions:
-      id-token: write
       contents: read
       pull-requests: write
       issues: write
     steps:
+      - name: Mint GPT reviewer app token
+        id: app-token
+        uses: actions/create-github-app-token@bcd2ba49218906704ab6c1aa796996da409d3eb1 # v3.2.0
+        with:
+          client-id: ${{ vars.GPT_REVIEWER_CLIENT_ID }}
+          private-key: ${{ secrets.GPT_REVIEWER_PRIVATE_KEY }}
+
       - name: Checkout repository
         uses: actions/checkout@v6
         with:
-          # Persist the token in git config so the opencode action's own
-          # `git fetch origin` is authenticated (otherwise it fails with
-          # "could not read Username for 'https://github.com'").
           fetch-depth: 1
-          persist-credentials: true
+          token: ${{ steps.app-token.outputs.token }}
 
       - name: Review with GPT-5.5 (variant xhigh)
         uses: ./.github/actions/run-opencode-review
         with:
           model: openai/gpt-5.5
           variant: xhigh
           opencode-auth-json: ${{ secrets.OPENCODE_AUTH_JSON }}
-          github-token: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
+          github-token: ${{ steps.app-token.outputs.token }}
           prompt: |
-            Use the `thermo-nuclear-code-quality-review` skill to review it. When adding comments on GitHub, always do so in Korean.
+            Use the `thermo-nuclear-code-quality-review` skill to review it. Always answer in Korean — write BOTH your final summary answer and every comment/review you post on GitHub in Korean.
 
-  opus-review:
+  claude-review:
     # Only starts after gpt-review completes successfully.
     needs: gpt-review
     runs-on: ubuntu-latest
     permissions:
-      id-token: write
       contents: read
       pull-requests: write
       issues: write
     steps:
+      - name: Mint Claude reviewer app token
+        id: app-token
+        uses: actions/create-github-app-token@bcd2ba49218906704ab6c1aa796996da409d3eb1 # v3.2.0
+        with:
+          client-id: ${{ vars.CLAUDE_REVIEWER_CLIENT_ID }}
+          private-key: ${{ secrets.CLAUDE_REVIEWER_PRIVATE_KEY }}
+
       - name: Checkout repository
         uses: actions/checkout@v6
         with:
-          # Persist the token in git config so the opencode action's own
-          # `git fetch origin` is authenticated (otherwise it fails with
-          # "could not read Username for 'https://github.com'").
           fetch-depth: 1
-          persist-credentials: true
+          token: ${{ steps.app-token.outputs.token }}
 
       - name: Review with Claude Opus 4.8 (variant max)
         uses: ./.github/actions/run-opencode-review
         with:
           model: anthropic/claude-opus-4-8
           variant: max
           opencode-auth-json: ${{ secrets.OPENCODE_AUTH_JSON }}
-          github-token: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
+          github-token: ${{ steps.app-token.outputs.token }}
           prompt: |
-            Use the `thermo-nuclear-code-quality-review` skill to review it. First read the PR comments using the gh CLI. When adding comments on GitHub, always do so in Korean.
+            Use the `thermo-nuclear-code-quality-review` skill to review it. First read the existing PR comments using the gh CLI. Always answer in Korean — write BOTH your final summary answer and every comment/review you post on GitHub in Korean.

display.text:
name: review

on:
  pull_request:
    types: [opened, synchronize, reopened, ready_for_review]

# OPENCODE_AUTH_JSON is a repo secret and is not exposed to PRs from forks.
# These workflows are intended for same-repo PRs only.
jobs:
  gpt-review:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      id-token: write
      contents: read
      pull-requests: write
      issues: write
    steps:
      - name: Checkout repository
        uses: actions/checkout@v6
        with:
          # Persist the token in git config so the opencode action's own
          # `git fetch origin` is authenticated (otherwise it fails with
          # "could not read Username for 'https://github.com'").
          fetch-depth: 1
          persist-credentials: true

      - name: Review with GPT-5.5 (variant xhigh)
        uses: ./.github/actions/run-opencode-review
        with:
          model: openai/gpt-5.5
          variant: xhigh
          opencode-auth-json: ${{ secrets.OPENCODE_AUTH_JSON }}
          github-token: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
          prompt: |
            Use the `thermo-nuclear-code-quality-review` skill to review it. When adding comments on GitHub, always do so in Korean.

  opus-review:
    # Only starts after gpt-review completes successfully.
    needs: gpt-review
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      id-token: write
      contents: read
      pull-requests: write
      issues: write
    steps:
      - name: Checkout repository
        uses: actions/checkout@v6
        with:
          # Persist the token in git config so the opencode action's own
          # `git fetch origin` is authenticated (otherwise it fails with
          # "could not read Username for 'https://github.com'").
          fetch-depth: 1
          persist-credentials: true

      - name: Review with Claude Opus 4.8 (variant max)
        uses: ./.github/actions/run-opencode-review
        with:
          model: anthropic/claude-opus-4-8
          variant: max
          opencode-auth-json: ${{ secrets.OPENCODE_AUTH_JSON }}
          github-token: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
          prompt: |
            Use the `thermo-nuclear-code-quality-review` skill to review it. First read the PR comments using the gh CLI. When adding comments on GitHub, always do so in Korean.

state.title: .github/workflows/review.yml

parts.text: I'm rewriting the workflow file to integrate an app token for authentication, updating the checkout step to use that token, renaming the review job, removing unnecessary OIDC permissions, and adding Korean language prompts with a header comment explaining the app's purpose.

input.content:
name: review

on:
  pull_request:
    types: [opened, synchronize, reopened, ready_for_review]

# OPENCODE_AUTH_JSON is a repo secret and is not exposed to PRs from forks.
# These workflows are intended for same-repo PRs only.
#
# Each job mints its own GitHub App installation token so review comments post
# under a distinct bot identity (hena-gpt-reviewer[bot] / hena-claude-reviewer[bot]).
# The app token is also used for checkout so the opencode action's own
# `git fetch origin` is authenticated.
jobs:
  gpt-review:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      contents: read
      pull-requests: write
      issues: write
    steps:
      - name: Mint GPT reviewer app token
        id: app-token
        uses: actions/create-github-app-token@bcd2ba49218906704ab6c1aa796996da409d3eb1 # v3.2.0
        with:
          client-id: ${{ vars.GPT_REVIEWER_CLIENT_ID }}
          private-key: ${{ secrets.GPT_REVIEWER_PRIVATE_KEY }}

      - name: Checkout repository
        uses: actions/checkout@v6
        with:
          fetch-depth: 1
          token: ${{ steps.app-token.outputs.token }}

      - name: Review with GPT-5.5 (variant xhigh)
        uses: ./.github/actions/run-opencode-review
        with:
          model: openai/gpt-5.5
          variant: xhigh
          opencode-auth-json: ${{ secrets.OPENCODE_AUTH_JSON }}
          github-token: ${{ steps.app-token.outputs.token }}
          prompt: |
            Use the `thermo-nuclear-code-quality-review` skill to review it. Always answer in Korean — write BOTH your final summary answer and every comment/review you post on GitHub in Korean.

  claude-review:
    # Only starts after gpt-review completes successfully.
    needs: gpt-review
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      contents: read
      pull-requests: write
      issues: write
    steps:
      - name: Mint Claude reviewer app token
        id: app-token
        uses: actions/create-github-app-token@bcd2ba49218906704ab6c1aa796996da409d3eb1 # v3.2.0
        with:
          client-id: ${{ vars.CLAUDE_REVIEWER_CLIENT_ID }}
          private-key: ${{ secrets.CLAUDE_REVIEWER_PRIVATE_KEY }}

      - name: Checkout repository
        uses: actions/checkout@v6
        with:
          fetch-depth: 1
          token: ${{ steps.app-token.outputs.token }}

      - name: Review with Claude Opus 4.8 (variant max)
        uses: ./.github/actions/run-opencode-review
        with:
          model: anthropic/claude-opus-4-8
          variant: max
          opencode-auth-json: ${{ secrets.OPENCODE_AUTH_JSON }}
          github-token: ${{ steps.app-token.outputs.token }}
          prompt: |
            Use the `thermo-nuclear-code-quality-review` skill to review it. First read the existing PR comments using the gh CLI. Always answer in Korean — write BOTH your final summary answer and every comment/review you post on GitHub in Korean.

Since the new branch inherits the prior commits from `chore/opencode-ci-review`, PR #2 will show all the project files plus the updated workflow — plenty of content for review.

input.content:
## Summary

Re-test of the opencode PR review workflow, now with **distinct GitHub App identities** per model and **Korean-only output**. Supersedes #1.

- **Per-model bot identity** — each job mints its own GitHub App installation token via `actions/create-github-app-token` (pinned to v3.2.0) and posts comments under a distinct bot:
  - `gpt-review` → `hena-gpt-reviewer[bot]`
  - `claude-review` → `hena-claude-reviewer[bot]`
- The app token is also passed to `actions/checkout` so the opencode action's own `git fetch origin` is authenticated.
- **Korean everywhere** — both prompts now require Korean for the final summary answer *and* every GitHub comment/review.
- Renamed the `opus-review` job to `claude-review`; dropped the unused `id-token: write` permission.
- Sequencing unchanged: `claude-review` runs only after `gpt-review` succeeds.

## Verification

This PR triggers the workflow. Expected: both jobs succeed, comments appear under the two distinct bot authors, and all output is in Korean.

state.title: Search claude-review failed log for the error

parts.text:
The `create-github-app-token` action failed because the Claude reviewer app isn't installed on the `hena-agent/hena` repository—when it tried to fetch the app's installation details from GitHub's API, it got a 404 error.

The GPT reviewer app worked fine (it successfully posted a comment), which means it's properly installed on the repo. So the issue is clear: the Claude app was created and configured with the credentials provided, but the installation step on the repository was either skipped or never completed. The user needs to manually install the "Hena Claude Reviewer" app on the `hena-agent/hena` repository through GitHub's UI.

Let me verify the diagnosis by checking the GPT reviewer's comment to confirm it's working correctly with the right identity and language settings.

Diagnosed. The `claude-review` job failed at the **token step**, not the review:

```
GET https://api.github.com/repos/hena-agent/hena/installation → 404 Not Found
```

That means the **"Hena Claude Reviewer" App isn't installed on the `hena-agent/hena` repo** (the GPT App is — `gpt-review` succeeded). Let me confirm the GPT side fully worked (distinct bot + Korean) so we know only the Claude App install is missing.

---

**Translation (Korean):**

진단 완료. `claude-review` 작업은 리뷰 단계가 아니라 **토큰 단계**에서 실패했습니다.

```
GET https://api.github.com/repos/hena-agent/hena/installation → 404 Not Found
```

이는 **"Hena Claude Reviewer" 앱이 `hena-agent/hena` 저장소에 설치되어 있지 않다**는 뜻입니다(GPT 앱은 설치되어 있으며, `gpt-review`는 성공했습니다). Claude 앱 설치만 누락된 것인지 확인할 수 있도록 GPT 쪽이 완전히 정상 작동했는지(별도 봇 + 한국어) 확인해 보겠습니다.
